---
title: About
description: "About iSRL — Interdisciplinary Systems Research Lab building open data infrastructure for Indian packaged food."
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-location: right
    reference-location: margin
    filters:
    - highlight-span.lua
    css: styles.css
---

```{=html}
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "AboutPage",
  "name": "About iSRL",
  "description": "About iSRL — Interdisciplinary Systems Research Lab building open data infrastructure for Indian packaged food.",
  "url": "https://isrl-research.github.io/about.html",
  "about": {
    "@type": "Organization",
    "name": "iSRL",
    "alternateName": "Interdisciplinary Systems Research Lab",
    "url": "https://isrl-research.github.io",
    "description": "Open research lab building IFID — the data coordination layer for Indian packaged food ingredient identity."
  }
}
</script>
```

*Ya Devi Sarva Bhuteshu Buddhi Roopena Samsthita,*
*Namasthasyai Namo Namah*

Salutations to the Goddess who dwells in all beings in the form of intelligence.
I bow to her again and again.

This is the founding premise of the lab. Not decoration — the philosophical
chairperson of everything we build.

---

## What iSRL is

iSRL is an open research commons. Independent — not affiliated with any
university, not beholden to any institution's research agenda. We pick one
problem at a time and go deep, producing work that is open not just in its
outputs but in its process: the debates, the dead ends, the decisions, the
things that didn't work. All of it happens in public, on GitHub.

Most research is output-open. A paper gets published, a dataset gets released,
the CC BY licence gets attached. The messy middle — how the question was framed,
what was tried before the answer emerged, where the reasoning failed — stays
internal. We don't think that's the right model for research that is meant to
serve as public infrastructure. If the work is meant to be used, the work needs
to be understood. That means showing the process, not just the product.

Our 2026 focus is the Indian Food Informatics Data project — building the
coordination layer that makes ingredient identity in Indian packaged food
queryable, interoperable, and stable. The goal is coordination without
convergence: a system that lets thousands of regional ingredient expressions talk
to regulatory, trade, and health systems without flattening the diversity that
makes them meaningful.

---

## How it is structured

The lab runs on layered coordination.

**Core researchers** hold the system across sprints. They think about the
architecture — the classification frameworks, the governance principles, the
infrastructure that makes domain work possible. They scope the problems, maintain
the pipelines, and ensure that what one sprint produces is something the next
sprint can build on. This is domain-agnostic work: the question is always how
the system should hold, not what any one ingredient or regulation says.

**Domain contributors** come in where their expertise intersects a live problem.
A food scientist on fortification. A legal researcher on a court ruling. A
nutritionist on allergen evidence. They don't need to understand the whole
system — they need to understand their piece of it precisely. The core creates
the conditions for that contribution to land cleanly; the domain expert produces
something the system can integrate.

The interface between them is the research problem itself — scoped precisely
enough for a domain expert to engage with, situated clearly enough in the larger
system that the contribution has a home.

---

## How we work

Fully async. Fully remote. No office, no fixed hours, no check-ins for the sake
of check-ins.

What we do keep is a deliberate rhythm. Every third week is off — completely.
No updates, no pings, no gentle nudges. That week exists because ideas need time
to settle, and because the people doing this work have lives that deserve
uninterrupted space. Some of the most useful realisations happen when you stop
pushing.

Work weeks are high agency. You know what the deliverable is. You organise your
time around it. No one is managing your hours.

We are not interested in the appearance of productivity. We are interested in
the work holding up.

---

## The culture underneath

The lab operates on one premise that doesn't move: your worth as a person has
nothing to do with your output, your research record, or anything you produce
here. You are not here to impress anyone. You are here to serve the truth as
faithfully as you can and represent it as clearly as possible.

**Put yourself first.** Before you commit to anything here, ask whether you will
still get enough sleep, enough rest, enough of your own life. We mean this
without qualification. Opportunities will always exist. Your wellbeing is not
renewable on demand.

**Say no early.** A clear no upfront is worth more to us than a reluctant yes
that slowly unravels. We actively create space for saying no. A half-committed
presence is harder to work around than an honest absence.

**Full commitment or none.** When you do say yes, we expect the real version of
that. Not perfection — commitment. Showing up, communicating when things aren't
working, doing the unglamorous parts without being asked.

**Serve the truth.** Your job is not to defend a position or make the lab look
good. It is to find out what is actually true, document it clearly, and be
willing to say when the answer is inconvenient. That is the whole job.

---

## Before you show up

Some honest questions worth sitting with:

- Can you get enough sleep on a working week and still meet your commitments
  here?
- Are you comfortable working without someone checking in on you?
- Can you communicate clearly — not just when things are going well, but when
  they aren't?
- Are you able to say "I don't know" and then go find out?
- Is there space in your life right now for something that requires your full
  attention?

If the honest answer to any of these is no right now, that is fine. The timing
has to be right. Come back when it is.

If the problem is pulling at you and the timing is right — the
[discussions](https://github.com/orgs/isrl-research/discussions) are open. No
form, no application. Just come in.
